title: Reminiscence Bingo
shortDescription: A memory-focused bingo activity that encourages residents to share stories and life experiences.

parts.text: On Thursday, June 18th, I visited Magnolia Memory Care of Flower Mound and brought reminiscence bingo with me. To create the bingo cards, I used the bingo card generator on
. In the generator, I added 50 questions, and the website created 30 different versions of the bingo cards. After that, I printed and cut out the bingo cards.
You can access the 30 bingo cards as well as the caller's card

content.text: One thing that I noticed was that the bingo cards were a little too small for the residents, so it would be better to double the bingo card size currently on the sheet. Additionally, a lot of the residents that I was working with had difficulty reading, so the staff and I had to read the cards out to them.

Initially, I was planning on making the game rules such that I would call out one of the squares on the caller's card, and the residents would look on the sheet for the square. If they have the square on their card, they would share with the others their answer to that question. However, I would only recommend this method if you are working with residents who are more mobile and comfortable with reading.

Therefore, instead, the activity director and I split up the residents, and we read the questions on the bingo cards out to them. It is important to always be encouraging and positive when a resident says they do not know or remember something. This can help prevent them from feeling sadness related to their memory.

In the end, the residents enjoyed talking about their life experiences, even if they were sometimes not able to recall something. This activity helps the residents cognitively because it stretches their mind to remember and talk about something from a long time ago. I suggest this activity for all types of senior living centers.

title: Photo Trivia
shortDescription: A visual trivia activity that encourages residents to identify familiar objects while practicing active recall and short-term memory.

content.text: On Thursday, June 18th, I visited Magnolia Memory Care of Flower Mound and brought 20 different trivia cards with me. To create the trivia cards, I printed photos of random objects on half sheets of paper. I felt that this was a good paper size for the residents to see. I chose some objects that they would see in the present day and other objects that they would recognize from the past.
You can view a PDF of the images that I used for the trivia cards in the downloadable materials below.

The residents became very happy when they correctly identified what was shown on a trivia card, and we encouraged them by cheering and clapping. This provided positive reinforcement, which can be extremely beneficial for aging populations.

Something I would do differently would be to bring even more cards. The residents were having fun, and we ended up repeating some of the cards after we ran out. One thing I noticed was that when residents did not know the name of an object the first time, some of them remembered it when the card appeared again.

This activity can help improve short-term memory because residents engage in active recall, which may be helpful when addressing the effects of cognitive decline. I suggest this activity for all types of senior living centers.

title: Color Match Noodle Game
shortDescription: A color-recognition game that combines cognitive recall, physical movement, and friendly competition using colored pool noodles.

content.text: On Friday, June 19th, I visited Magnolia Memory Care of Flower Mound, and the activity director and I placed five pool noodles on each table. After that, I set up a color picker using an online picker wheel. However, after a few rounds, I realized that the picker was not necessary, and we began choosing colors randomly without it.
Each time I called out a color, the residents raced to choose the matching pool noodle. The resident who selected the correct noodle the fastest was the winner.
One thing I noticed was that the residents did not always hear me the first time I announced a color. The activity director gave me a microphone, which helped them hear the instructions more clearly.

I also noticed that some residents had difficulty picking up the noodles. For example, even when one resident heard me and selected the correct noodle, he placed his finger on it instead of picking it up. This may have been related to difficulties with motor planning or task initiation. We still made sure to clap and cheer for him after he pointed to the correct noodle.

Some residents also had difficulty distinguishing between similar colors. For instance, several residents thought that the pink pool noodle was red, so they were unsure which noodle to choose when I called out "pink." There was also a teal pool noodle that residents frequently confused with blue, so we eventually removed it from the game.

This activity can support residents cognitively because they must recall colors and perform a physical action based on the information they hear. The residents enjoyed the activity, so I suggest it for all types of senior living centers. For a more mobile group of residents, you may also tally points and keep score to add a competitive element to the game.

title: Aquatic Tape-Painting
shortDescription: A supervised painting activity that uses taped aquatic shapes to support fine motor skills, color recall, and creative expression.

content.text: On Tuesday, June 23rd, I visited Magnolia Memory Care of Flower Mound, and Ms. Abbi, the activity director, came up with the idea to cut small shapes of aquatic creatures from masking tape and place them on mini canvases. We prepared one turtle shape and one shark shape.
We brought the canvases to the residents and prepared paint palettes and brushes for them. Since this works best as a one-on-one activity, we worked with one or two residents during each round.

It is important to supervise residents closely during this activity. Some residents had previously attempted to eat paint, so washable, non-toxic finger paint intended for young children would be safer than acrylic paint that may contain toxic ingredients.

I also noticed that some residents did not understand what was on the palette and touched the paint with their fingers. One resident became upset after getting paint on her hands and began wiping it underneath the table and on the floor. Wet wipes should be kept nearby so residents' hands can be cleaned quickly if needed.

This activity can support color and animal-name recall. I asked residents which colors they wanted to use and what animal was shown on their canvas. The amount of guidance can be adjusted based on each resident's abilities and personality.

Some residents with limited mobility needed help holding the brush, while residents with stronger fine motor skills were able to blend colors and create longer brush strokes. Newspaper or plastic wrap should be placed underneath the canvases to protect the table.

The paint took approximately 15 to 20 minutes to dry. Once it had dried, we removed the masking tape to reveal the white aquatic design beneath the paint. We then showed the residents their completed artwork and hung the paintings in their rooms.
Overall, this was a beneficial activity that encouraged creative expression, developed fine motor skills, and helped residents recall the names of colors and animals.

title: Connect the Dots: Hedgehog
shortDescription: A personalized connect-the-dots activity that supports number sequencing, active recall, conversation, and creative expression.

content.text: On Tuesday, June 23rd, I visited Magnolia Memory Care of Flower Mound and brought several connect-the-dots sheets. I first gave each resident a sheet and asked them to draw lines by following the numbers in order.
I used a hedgehog connect-the-dots worksheet. It was important to print only one hedgehog per sheet of paper so the numbers were large enough for the residents to read.

Some residents needed help remembering which number came next, while others completed the number sequence quickly. After each resident finished connecting the dots, I wrote their name in the center of the hedgehog.

I then asked personalized questions, such as their favorite fruit, favorite place to visit, or favorite activity. I wrote each response inside the hedgehog. This added emotional engagement and made every resident's artwork unique.

Finally, I colored a blue sky and green grass and lightly colored around the words inside the hedgehog. I then asked each resident to identify the animal. Many residents called it a rat, so I explained that it was a hedgehog.
To practice active recall, I asked residents again a few minutes later to see whether they remembered the animal's name.
Overall, this activity supported number sequencing, memory recall, conversation, and personalization, making it both cognitively engaging and meaningful for residents.

downloads.title: Download the Hedgehog Connect the Dots Sheet

title: Seashell Montessori Matching
shortDescription: A simple matching activity that helps residents practice visual recognition, reading, pattern matching, and memory.

content.text: On Thursday, June 25th, I visited Magnolia Memory Care of Flower Mound, and Ms. Abbi, the activity director, came up with the idea to use a seashell Montessori activity to engage the residents.

Although seniors and toddlers are at opposite ends of the human lifespan, some memory care residents may benefit from activities that use similarly simple matching, sorting, and recognition tasks. For this reason, certain Montessori-style activities can be adapted for senior living communities.

The activity included a set of cards showing different types of seashells and matching sheets containing approximately 10 to 12 seashell images. We gave residents the cards and asked them to match each card to the corresponding picture on the sheet.

Some residents were especially interested in the seashells and commented that they were pretty. After helping them match the cards to the images, I held up a new card and asked the resident to identify the name of the seashell.

To answer, the resident needed to look at the matching sheet, locate the corresponding seashell, and read its name aloud. This encouraged visual matching, pattern recognition, reading, and memory recall.
Overall, this was a beneficial and simple activity that can be adapted for residents in many types of senior living communities.
